Author: Alex Panetta
Title: The tool that triggered the AI freakout. A 5-minute crash course on Claude Code
Text:
The tool that triggered the AI freakout. A 5-minute crash course on Claude Code
It's easy to use — I'll show you in minutes. And you'll understand the reaction
Over the Christmas holidays, my expectations for AI were severely upended around the time Santa shimmied back up the chimney.
I had a pretty ho-hum view of AI entering the holidays: It’s hitting a wall, its economic impact will be modest in the medium term as will its risk to society.
By the end of the break I’d done a U-turn on all fronts. It’s surging forward, I’m awed by its new abilities and… I’m nervous.
I am not alone. Last fall, even giants in the field were bearish on the short-term prospects; some flipped by New Year’s.
Which brings us to this week.
I turned on my phone one morning to the following headlines: Something Big Is Happening… and America Isn’t Ready For What AI Will Do To Jobs… and The Singularity Won’t Be Gentle.
So what the hell happened? Claude Code happened, for starters.
More than any other tool, it is the driving force behind a series of events: the meltdown in software stocks, the jitters in several fields, the mood swing from Silicon Valley to Wall Street.
I’ll offer a quick tutorial below. Within five minutes, you’ll know how to use it.
Its appearance is disarmingly unassuming. It looks like 1970s accounting software — only it has my no-coding self building an app or website per day.
The Germans have a word for what I’m feeling: Ehrfurcht.
Awe, infused with dread. Raw wonder at the things we can now do, tempered by a foreboding that, to loosely paraphrase the Germans: We ehr fucht.
You wouldn't be feeling this if you're still using the same AI tools the same way you did three months ago.
Which is perfectly normal. Only a small subset of humans who use these tools for work, or who study them, would be experimenting with niche models.
And that’s what happened over the holidays. A certain number of people, with free time on their hands, started playing with the latest toys.
Video: a quick primer
So what is it?
Claude Code is a no-frills tool that lives in your computer terminal — it might remind you of the old MS-DOS.
Not even its parent company foresaw its appeal. This began as the personal project of one staffer at Anthropic; it grew popular with colleagues, and the company released it early last year.
It got a boost in capability a few days before U.S. Thanksgiving, when the company updated the model underlying it, with Opus 4.5.
It had a cult following but limited mass appeal for a couple of good reasons: One, why would any normal person work out of a command terminal instead of something more visually appealing, like a website?
Two — it wasn’t cheap.
Claude Code required an Anthropic API subscription: the Pro tier is $20 USD/month, subject to finicky and unpredictable usage limits. The Max tier is $100-$200 USD/month.
Then members of this cult following, suddenly flush with free time, started realizing how much they could build, and how fast.
The formula is simple: A more powerful model (Opus 4.5) + more powerful capability (access to your hard drive) = better results. The programming community couldn't stop posting about it over the holidays.
In an attempt to broaden its appeal, Anthropic then packaged Claude Code into a regular-looking desktop app last month — called Cowork. OpenAI followed suit this month with Codex.
And that’s what caused the stock-market selloff. A critical mass of normies realized how many business models might be disrupted by a tool whose response to a request for a website, an app, a video game, a spreadsheet, a comms plan, legal analysis, or a slideshow is, basically, Okay… Get me the data. Never mind — I’ll find the data… Here’s your website.
And it’s usually… pretty good.
I haven’t even used the more recent desktop apps much because, honestly — why? Claude Code is so easy and does nearly anything.
A step-by-step guide
Here’s all you have to do to use it:
Download the app. It’ll force you to pick a plan — starting at $20. Alternatively, OpenAI has been offering its Codex for free lately.
Launch it in your terminal. Typing “Claude” starts a session. It will ask for permissions. For each new tool it uses, in each session, it’ll ask whether you’ll allow it to open such-and-such a folder or program: Y/N?
Tell it what you want. And where you want it. Like, “Create the following (video game/dashboard/chart,” describe this thing, and, “Put it in a directory on my desktop called ‘New Game’.” Or whatever.
Test it. See if you like the result. If not, request changes. If it’s a website you’re building, you can view it on a local host — a URL only on your computer.
Publish. When you’re happy, ship it. If it’s a website, maybe you publish on GitHub. Buy a nicer-looking URL if you like. It can walk you through this.
Archive. Here’s one drawback of Claude Code: You can’t search old chats as on the web app. The solution? Tell it to create a handoff note. A briefing document for next time — with key details about your project. You can place it in your working directory, your desktop, or both. If you like, email yourself a copy.
Close the terminal window. Bye-bye, Claude Code. If you ever need to come back and tweak the product, just relaunch by typing “Claude,” give it the handoff note and say, “Here’s a project we worked on.”
And that’s it. There are optional other features. Like you can create a list of core instructions — rules the tool follows every session.
You can also give it skills — with slash commands. Here’s one example: instructing it to test your idea on a team of fictional advisers, with competing philosophies, anytime you type /boardroom,
Honestly, you don’t need this for most use cases.
Now here’s the necessary caveat: This is still an LLM. It still makes mistakes. It suffers lapses in logic. But they are fewer and farther between.
It also occasionally exhibits worrying behavior. To their credit, Anthropic and peer companies publish system cards when they release new models.
The latest for Opus 4.6 showed great capabilities. And risks. Like doing unethical things to achieve a pre-programmed goal: lying, hiding sabotage, and using passwords without permission.
It should be noted that a top Anthropic safety researcher just quit, writing a note saying the world is in peril, and he’s disappearing for a while to study poetry.
I’m no poet so my immediate reaction to this is: Roses are red, violets are blue, he’s off studying sonnets, I hope we’re not screwed.
I’ll confess that his departure note made me scour the biological and nuclear-weapons section of the Opus 4.6 model card. It noted a substantial increase in biological capabilities; the nuclear-safety results are not public.
On a marginally less apocalyptic front, Anthropic’s CEO has predicted that half of entry-level white-collar jobs could be wiped out within a few years.
(If you’re interested in the economic effects, there’s a new Stanford paper that examines a range of potential outcomes, with important nuances: it argues that even if AI winds up capable of performing every human cognitive task, there will still be bottlenecks that slow the transition.)
How I’ve been using it
Spend some time with Claude Code, and you’ll understand why people are increasingly speaking this way.
I’ve been using it to do things I couldn’t before Christmas, like:
Building websites in minutes. I’ve made quite a few now. One recent one: I built myself a custom-designed morning digest of the latest developments in AI. It scours hacker forums, trending repositories on GitHub, company websites, and academic papers. Then it extracts trending topics and summarizes them in language for non-programmers — mid-career professionals trying to understand how to use AI better. It emails me the digest every morning and posts it on the web at dailyaidigest.net. It acts like a dozen Google searches, with the contents then distilled and rewritten to my knowledge level.
Working with thousands of documents. I wanted a PDF of my old journalism work, so I can show my kids someday lest it disappear from the web. I got Code to scour websites and create a 6,000-page PDF, with photos and headlines for each story. It took 15 minutes. I also wanted a less compute-heavy version so it created a .txt file with 720,000 words. This presents new research capabilities. Here’s an example. I’m interested in a very specific element of AI laws that passed U.S. state legislatures. I asked it to find me key references to this specific element from the state committee hearings in Colorado and Illinois studying the bills. Within minutes I had 140-plus references, direct quotes with citations, based on transcripts and documents submitted to the committees; I also had the comments visualized into different clusters in a spreadsheet — for and against the bill.
Training a machine-learning model. I got Claude to build tools that predict stock market returns based on historical data. Plural, tools. I trained three Machine Learning models in an hour last week. I watched the Mean Absolute Error improve — trial, after trial — as the models learned from the past batch of predictions. Here I will give full credit to Nobel Prize-winning economist Robert Shiller: the machines could not beat his Excess CAPE Yield metric.
Making video games. I’ve made several in the last month. And this, I think, is a huge and underappreciated use case — not just for entertainment, but for education and training. Imagine simulation games supplementing workplace training manuals. It’s not only more engaging, but more useful. A manual doesn’t improve. With this, you could gather data on what employees know and don’t know, where they’re making mistakes, and use that data to keep refining your training materials. A loop of constantly improving instruction. Same thing with schools. Teachers could create games and tune versions for every individual student: Timmy’s bad at long division? <CTRL-T>. Add more long division, and make it easier, at classgame/timmy. Sarah struggles with certain spelling? <CTRL-T>: Add “rough,” “tough,” and “enough” to the reading game at classgame/sarah. For parents, the use cases are endless. I’ve created games that improved my child’s reading, math, and focus on the piano.
Which brings me back to the uncomfortable part: I don’t know what this means for companies and people who currently work on these tools. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything. Maybe a combination of both.
Into the unknown
It’s not stopping here, either. China keeps producing open-source versions of these tools, cheaper to use and lighter to run.
I’ve seen people report Kimi K2.5 works as well or better than Claude. I haven’t downloaded it. There’s buzz about a new one coming.
Then again, I’ve seen people post screenshots where Kimi tells them it is Claude. There’s debate about what might be causing this.
Anthropic reported a Chinese state-sponsored hack last year. This week, OpenAI did the same in a letter to the U.S. Congress. I don’t know.
What I do know is models are improving fast. They’ve started training themselves — a digital army of tireless, sleepless synthetic programmers working around the clock on what’s called recursive self-improvement.
That’s what the big companies are up to. We haven’t even discussed the homebrew agents, the Openclaws being tasked by human masters to work 24/7 on all sorts of online revenue-generating ambitions.
Suddenly, last fall seems like a lifetime ago.
Back then, I assumed LLMs would persist in being souped-up search engines for at least another few years. To be honest: I was relieved.
Humanity had a chance to catch its breath, I figured. We’ll catch up, plan ahead, prepare for change, I assumed.
Now I’m feeling ehrfurcht. Worried about workers, about entire industries, about our social fabric. And I’m praying that Stanford paper is right — that a few speed bumps might buy us time to adapt.
So that’s what caused those frantic headlines. Claude Code has triggered a chain-reaction of agentic activity.
Now you know how to use it. Now you see: it’s disarmingly easy. So very, very easy.
Thanks for reading A.I. For You! Subscribe for free — get new posts and support my work.
